raw_premise: design a short study to understand how BLV screen-reader users interpret page hierarchy on AI-generated news articles
sharpest_question: What does 'AI-generated' do to the page hierarchy that a conventionally-authored news article would not — and if the answer is 'nothing structural', why is this a study about AI at all rather than a study about news-article hierarchy?
claim: BLV screen-reader users' interpretation of page hierarchy differs in some characterizable way when the underlying article is AI-generated, and that difference has implications for accessibility practice or authoring tools.
differentia: Not visible in the premise. The premise bundles three independent variables — BLV population, screen-reader modality, AI provenance — without specifying which contrast is load-bearing. Without that, this reduces to one of several existing templates.
nearest_template: Qualitative/think-aloud studies of screen-reader navigation on web content (Mack et al., Bigham lab, WebAIM survey tradition), typically reporting heading-level confusion, landmark mis-use, and skim strategies. 'AI-generated' is a surface substitution on a well-worn protocol.
missing_evidence: No pilot data or informal observation suggesting AI-generated articles have systematically different hierarchy from human-authored ones (flatter? deeper? mislabeled headings? hallucinated section structure?).
No specification of what 'interpret' means operationally — navigation time, mental model reconstruction, trust judgments, content recall, or something else.
No control condition named. AI-generated vs. what? Human-authored from the same outlet? Same topic? Matched length?
No account of how participants would know or not know the article is AI-generated, and whether that disclosure is part of the manipulation.
No sample size reasoning or recruitment plan for a population that is expensive to recruit and over-studied for low-stakes questions.
No stated stakeholder — is this for screen-reader users, for news organizations deploying LLMs, for accessibility tooling, or for AI model developers?
sharpened_options: Audit-first study: corpus-analyze hierarchy structure (heading depth, landmark use, ARIA correctness) across AI-generated vs. human-authored articles from matched outlets, then run a small user study only on the dimensions where the audit shows real divergence. Grounds the user study in a demonstrated artifact difference.

Trust-and-provenance study: hold hierarchy roughly constant and vary disclosure of AI authorship; measure whether BLV screen-reader users' navigation strategies and credibility judgments shift. The claim becomes about disclosure and auditory reading, not hierarchy per se.

Repair-tool formative study: observe where BLV screen-reader users break down on AI-generated articles specifically (e.g., hallucinated headings, inconsistent landmark nesting), and use findings to specify an accessibility-repair layer for LLM news pipelines. Premise becomes design-oriented with a concrete artifact target.

Comparative mental-model study: have BLV screen-reader users reconstruct article structure after listening, compare against sighted readers and against ground-truth DOM; test whether AI-generated articles produce larger reconstruction error for screen-reader users than for sighted readers. The contribution is a measured interaction effect, not a descriptive account.
